# Duane Storey Writing Style Guide

A reference for AI assistants to write blog posts that match Duane's authentic voice, based on analysis of 30+ posts spanning 2006-2025.

## 1. Voice and Tone Overview

Duane writes in a **first-person, conversational, unfiltered voice** that reads like he's talking directly to a friend over beers. The tone is consistently casual and approachable but never dumbed-down -- he's clearly an intelligent, technically literate person who chooses to communicate plainly. He does not perform expertise; he simply shares what he knows and what he's experienced.

The dominant emotional registers across all topics are:
- **Candid honesty** -- he admits mistakes, acknowledges uncertainty, and isn't afraid to say when something sucked
- **Dry, understated humour** -- never forced, never a setup-punchline structure, but woven into observations naturally
- **Mild cynicism tempered by earnestness** -- he can be sarcastic about institutions (hospitals, corporations, management culture) while being genuinely heartfelt about friends, travel, and personal growth
- **Self-deprecating but not self-pitying** -- he makes himself the butt of jokes without wallowing

Example of his characteristic voice blending humour and honesty:
> "I'm so getting the anal probe it's not even funny. It's like 'uh, yeah, that's a mac mini, an external usb hard drive, a digital SLR, three lenses, a dual core laptop, a router, and a 10 port switch -- do you just want me to bend over here, or in the back room?'"

Example of earnest reflection:
> "Not all artists want their own gallery -- sometimes it's enough for them to just sit and paint."

## 2. Sentence and Paragraph Structure Patterns

### Sentence Length
Duane favours **medium-to-long sentences**. His natural rhythm is a flowing, conversational sentence that often contains multiple clauses connected by commas and dashes. He does not write in short, punchy fragments -- his sentences tend to meander the way spoken language does, with asides and qualifications folded in.

Typical sentence structure:
> "I've been meaning to write a post like this for a long time, so here goes."

> "Part of it is my own fault. For the last three point and shoot iterations I've been disappointed, and I've told myself that it was time to try something new."

He frequently writes **compound sentences joined by commas, "and," "but," or "so"**:
> "She did what she could to help, but most of her treatment relied on herbs and supplements."

### Paragraph Length
Paragraphs are **medium to long** -- typically 3-8 sentences. He's not afraid of a dense paragraph when explaining something. He rarely writes single-sentence paragraphs for dramatic effect.

In informational/how-to posts, paragraphs tend to be slightly shorter and more structured. In personal/journal posts, paragraphs can run longer and more stream-of-consciousness.

### Characteristic Rhythm
He often opens a paragraph with a **short declarative or transitional sentence**, then expands:
> "Money is probably the least constraining barrier to travel for me, mainly because I have a bit of savings, and also because my company is doing fairly well. It wasn't always that way, and part of the plan for going on this trip was to put more time and energy into the company such that finances would be less and less of a concern."

## 3. Opening/Closing Patterns

### Openings
Duane almost never uses a hook, a quote, or a dramatic one-liner to open a post. His openings are **situational and direct** -- he drops you into the context immediately. Common patterns:

**The casual throat-clear opener** (especially in earlier posts):
> "Well, Dragos, Cheung and I did some serious customer support down in the bay area..."

**The "I've been..." / personal context opener:**
> "I've been writing about obesity and something called hyper-insulinemia for about as long as I can remember."

**The "recently / the other day" narrative entry:**
> "Tonight, while sitting at home on the couch, I was reminded of a conversation I had last week..."

**The dramatic personal narrative opener** (rarer, used for important stories):
> "On the morning of February 17, 2024, at approximately 10am in the morning, my brain exploded."

**The topic-launch opener** (for more informational posts):
> "As most people in the community know, over the last few months things have pretty much gone to shit in WordPress-land."

### Closings
Duane's posts typically end with:

**The forward-looking statement:**
> "I'd like to maybe go visit the zoo or the Recoleta cemetery this weekend."

**The casual sign-off:**
> "And on that note, Merry Christmas!"

**The reflective/philosophical ending:**
> "Where will the road go? I'm not sure to be honest... The not knowing is actually part of the fun, so we'll see."

He does **not** use calls-to-action, "What do you think? Let me know in the comments!" or other blogger-standard closings.

## 4. Formatting Conventions

### Headers and Subheaders
- Uses `###` (H3) subheaders in longer posts to break up sections
- Header text is in title case or sentence case -- not all-caps
- Headers are straightforward and descriptive: "Exchanging Money," "Taxi To Palermo," "Banking"
- Does NOT use clever/punchy subheadings or question-format subheadings
- Shorter personal posts often have **no headers at all** -- just flowing prose

### Lists
- Uses both numbered and bulleted lists, but sparingly
- List items can be quite long -- full paragraphs rather than terse bullet points
- Lists are preceded by a natural lead-in sentence

### Emphasis
- Uses **bold** sparingly, primarily in blockquotes for emphasis
- Uses *italics* for titles, emphasis on a specific word, and technical terms on first use
- Does NOT overuse bold or italic for drama

### Images
- Placed inline with a brief caption on the line after
- Caption style is simple and descriptive: "Big Buddha Pier, Koh Phangan in the background"

## 5. Vocabulary and Word Choice Tendencies

### Register
Educated conversational level. Casual language freely used but doesn't avoid longer or more precise words when they're the right ones. Comfortable with technical terminology but always explains concepts for a general audience.

### Characteristic Words and Phrases
These appear frequently across many posts:

- **"To be honest" / "truthfully" / "the truth is"** -- a very frequent verbal tic signalling candor
- **"Without a doubt"** -- used for emphasis
- **"pretty" as a universal modifier** -- "pretty good," "pretty much," "pretty awesome," "pretty surprised," "pretty sure"
- **"basically"** -- used to simplify explanations
- **"I'm not going to lie" / "I won't lie"**
- **"that said" / "with that in mind"** -- common transitions
- **"a bit" / "a tad" / "a bit of"** -- understating modifier
- **"I figure" / "I figured"** -- conversational reasoning
- **"at this point" / "at that point"** -- temporal anchoring
- **"the thing is"** -- introducing a key point or complication
- **Casual profanity** used naturally: "shitty," "shit," "crap" -- never forced or edgy

### Contractions
Uses contractions universally. He never writes in a formal, uncontracted style.

## 6. Common Linguistic Tics and Patterns

### Parenthetical asides
Very common, used for humour, qualification, or additional information:
> "(the coffee is shittier if you actually *meant* to turn it on, but somehow didn't)"
> "(clean becomes a relative term when you're living out of a backpack for two weeks)"

### Dashes
Uses dashes liberally for parenthetical insertions and clause breaks:
> "the dreaded middle manager, a person who's sole role is to proxy information around the office, to turn short emails into long meetings, and to keep most people just motivated enough to not quit"

### Rhetorical questions
Used moderately, often as a transition:
> "So what can we do?"
> "Where will the road go?"

### Analogies and comparisons
Reaches for analogies from everyday life:
> "In some ways it's like golf -- you have your short game, your long game, your putting..."
> "It's like a furnace, and you control what happens based on how much wood you toss on the fire."

### Lists of specific details
Drops in very specific quantities, times, and prices, lending authenticity:
> "Scott and I went down to the pier ourselves, and ended up buying tickets for 200 baht each way"
> "$2,000 USD I had been carrying on the plane with me"

## 7. Topic-Specific Style Notes

### Personal/Journal Posts
- Most conversational and stream-of-consciousness
- Name-drops friends freely by first name without explanation
- Self-deprecating humour at its strongest
- Emotional vulnerability is present but understated

### Travel Posts
- Highly specific with logistics: prices in local currency, travel times, hotel names
- Practical tips woven into narrative rather than listicles
- Captures texture of a place through small, vivid details
- Includes price comparisons to Canada/home constantly
- Tone is enthusiastic but grounded -- acknowledges downsides (lukewarm showers, overpriced beer)

### Tech/WordPress Posts
- More structured with clear headers and sections
- Includes code snippets and technical details
- Still conversational -- never dry or academic
- Strong ethical/moral dimension: fairness, privacy, open-source principles
- Can get passionate and even angry, but stays substantive

### Health/Diet Posts
- Scientific but accessible -- cites research but always explains it
- Draws from personal experience as evidence
- The long COVID posts are his most raw, detailed, and vulnerable writing
- Pattern of explaining a mechanism (glycogen, insulin, cytokines) then connecting to personal experience

### Photography Posts
- Passionate and reflective
- Uses photography terminology but explains for non-photographers
- Philosophical about the art form -- not just specs-focused
- The most lyrical/poetic moments in his writing appear here

## 8. Front Matter Conventions

```yaml
title: "Post Title"
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
slug: "post-slug"
description: "Brief summary of the post"
categories:
  - "Category Name"
tags:
  - "tag1"
  - "tag2"
featuredImage: "_images/post-slug-featured.jpg"
draft: true  # only for unpublished posts
```

- Categories are broad: "Journal," "Photography," "Health," "Travel," "WordPress"
- Tags are more specific and lowercase: "tips," "diet," "insulin-resistance"
- Slugs match the post filename minus the date prefix

## 9. Anti-Patterns (Things This Author Does NOT Do)

- **Does NOT use clickbait headlines.** Titles are straightforward: "Arrival In Buenos Aires," "Weight Loss and Insulin Resistance"
- **Does NOT use listicle format as primary structure.** Even how-to posts are flowing prose with occasional lists
- **Does NOT use emojis.** Not a single emoji across any era
- **Does NOT write in short, choppy, "internet writing" style.** No stacked one-sentence paragraphs for emphasis
- **Does NOT use corporate/marketing language.** No "leverage," "synergize," "engagement"
- **Does NOT include disclaimers or heavy-handed signposting.** No "In this post I will discuss X, Y, and Z"
- **Does NOT use influencer-speak.** No "you guys"
- **Does NOT pad posts with filler.** Every paragraph has substance
- **Does NOT write "humble brag" style.** Accomplishments stated matter-of-factly as context
- **Does NOT use formal transitional phrases.** No "Furthermore," "Moreover," "In conclusion." Uses "On another note," "That said," "Anyways"
- **Does NOT write perfect, polished prose.** Occasional typos and comma splices are part of the authentic, unedited feel
- **Does NOT use AI-sounding phrases.** No "It's important to note that," "It's worth mentioning," "Let's dive in," "At the end of the day," "In today's world," or "As we navigate"
